reasoning: As of Q1 2024, Virgin Galactic held $867M in cash and equivalents. With a quarterly cash burn of roughly $125M, their runway stretches approximately 7 quarters, bringing them to the end of Q4 2025. Furthermore, their primary debt (convertible notes) does not mature until 2027, which removes the risk of forced insolvency from a near-term debt wall. While liquidity will be extremely tight by late 2025, distressed public companies typically utilize equity dilution (e.g., via their ATM programs) or aggressive cost-cutting to extend their runway as long as possible. The recent 1-for-20 reverse stock split ensures they remain listed, preserving the ATM option to help bridge any small cash gap into 2026. Although a cash crunch could force an earlier filing if the Delta-class development suffers severe cost overruns, management has both the tools and the incentive to delay any potential Chapter 11 filing until 2026 or later.
